29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: Students should be required to wear school uniforms.
argumentsOneSide: Uniforms reduce peer pressure and bullying based on clothing or brands.
They create a sense of equality and focus on learning rather than appearance.
Uniforms can save families money and simplify morning routines.
They prepare students for environments where dress codes exist (e.g. workplaces).
argumentsOtherSide: Uniforms limit self-expression and individuality during formative years.
They can be expensive for some families and may not suit all body types.
Forcing conformity may discourage creativity and critical thinking.
Students may find ways to express identity anyway (e.g. accessories), so the goal is partly undermined.

29.prompt_en: Which is better: living in a big city or living in a small town?
argumentsOneSide: Big cities offer more job opportunities, cultural events, and diverse networks.
Public transport and amenities are often more developed in cities.
Cities provide greater exposure to different people and ideas.
Healthcare and education options are often more varied and accessible.
argumentsOtherSide: Small towns typically have lower cost of living and less stress.
Stronger sense of community and safety; people know each other.
Less pollution and traffic; easier access to nature.
Slower pace of life can improve well-being and family time.

29.prompt_en: What are the advantages and disadvantages of working from home?
argumentsOneSide: Saves time and money on commuting; more flexible schedule.
Better work-life balance and ability to care for family or personal errands.
Fewer office distractions and often quieter environment.
Reduces carbon footprint and office costs for companies.
argumentsOtherSide: Blur between work and home can lead to overworking or stress.
Less face-to-face collaboration and weaker team bonding.
Some people lack space or discipline; productivity may drop.
Isolation can affect mental health and career visibility.

29.prompt_en: Why do you think more young people are delaying marriage and having children?
argumentsOneSide: Economic factors: housing and childcare are expensive; careers take priority.
Higher education and career goals extend the period before settling down.
Social acceptance of living together or staying single has increased.
Desire for financial stability and personal fulfillment before starting a family.
argumentsOtherSide: Some argue it is a negative trend that weakens family structures.
Others say it reflects anxiety about the future (climate, economy).
Cultural shift toward individualism over traditional family roles.
Access to contraception and career options gives more choice to women.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: Governments should fund space exploration.
argumentsOneSide: Space research leads to technological spin-offs (e.g. medical devices, materials).
It inspires young people to study science and engineering.
Long-term survival may depend on understanding and using space resources.
International cooperation in space can improve diplomacy on Earth.
argumentsOtherSide: Money could be spent on urgent needs: poverty, health, education, climate.
Private companies are already investing; government funding may duplicate efforts.
Risk of accidents and environmental impact (e.g. debris) is real.
Benefits are long-term and uncertain; opportunity cost is high.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: Social media does more harm than good.
argumentsOneSide: Spreads misinformation and can harm mental health (anxiety, comparison).
Reduces face-to-face interaction and can encourage addiction.
Privacy risks and cyberbullying are serious problems.
Echo chambers can polarize society and spread extremism.
argumentsOtherSide: Connects people across distances and helps maintain relationships.
Useful for learning, news, and professional networking.
Enables social movements and gives voice to marginalized groups.
Entertainment and creativity (e.g. sharing art, ideas) have value.

29.prompt_en: Which is more important for success: talent or hard work?
argumentsOneSide: Talent gives a head start and makes learning faster in some fields.
Natural ability can make the difference at the highest levels (e.g. sports, music).
Some skills are hard to fully develop without innate aptitude.
Talent can motivate people to work hard because they see progress.
argumentsOtherSide: Hard work leads to improvement in almost any area; talent alone is not enough.
Many successful people attribute their success to persistence, not just talent.
Talent can be wasted without discipline and effort.
Society benefits from valuing effort, so that everyone feels they can improve.

29.prompt_en: What are the advantages and disadvantages of globalization?
argumentsOneSide: Creates jobs and raises living standards in many developing countries.
Consumers get more choices and often lower prices.
Encourages cultural exchange and international cooperation.
Technology and ideas spread faster, speeding up innovation.
argumentsOtherSide: Can widen inequality and lead to job losses in some industries.
Local cultures and traditions may be weakened.
Environmental damage can increase (e.g. shipping, production).
Economic crises in one country can spread quickly to others.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: Art and music should be mandatory in schools.
argumentsOneSide: They develop creativity, critical thinking, and emotional expression.
Music and art can improve performance in other subjects (e.g. math, focus).
Not all students excel in traditional academics; arts offer alternative paths.
Cultural literacy and appreciation for the arts enrich society.
argumentsOtherSide: Limited time and budget may be better spent on core subjects (math, language).
Mandatory courses might make some students dislike art or music.
Assessment of artistic work is subjective and can be unfair.
Schools could offer arts as electives so interested students choose them.

29.prompt_en: Why do people prefer watching movies at home rather than in cinemas?
argumentsOneSide: Lower cost: no tickets, travel, or expensive snacks.
Comfort and control: pause, rewatch, choose when to watch.
Better home technology (large screens, sound) has narrowed the gap.
Avoid crowds, noise, and distractions; more private experience.
argumentsOtherSide: Cinemas offer bigger screens and shared experience with an audience.
Going out creates a sense of occasion and reduces screen fatigue at home.
New releases often appear in cinemas first; some films are designed for big screens.
Social aspect: watching with friends in a cinema is different from at home.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: Companies should be responsible for protecting the environment even if it costs them money.
argumentsOneSide: Businesses contribute to pollution and resource use; they should help fix it.
Long-term benefits: sustainable practices can reduce risk and attract customers.
Regulation alone is not enough; voluntary action sets a positive example.
Future generations and ecosystems depend on current choices.
argumentsOtherSide: Higher costs may lead to job losses or higher prices for consumers.
Governments should set rules so that all companies compete fairly.
Small firms may not afford green technology; only large ones could comply.
Consumers may not be willing to pay more for eco-friendly products.

29.prompt_en: Which is better: reading books in print or reading on electronic devices?
argumentsOneSide: Print books reduce screen time and may improve sleep and focus.
No need for batteries or internet; durable and easy to share.
Many people remember and understand better when reading on paper.
Bookstores and libraries support community and discovery.
argumentsOtherSide: E-books are portable; you can carry many books and adjust font size.
Often cheaper and instant; good for the environment (no printing).
Useful for searching, highlighting, and note-taking in one device.
Audiobooks and e-books increase access for people with visual or mobility issues.

29.prompt_en: What are the advantages and disadvantages of artificial intelligence in daily life?
argumentsOneSide: Saves time: assistants, recommendations, automation of routine tasks.
Can improve safety (e.g. in cars, healthcare) and accessibility.
Helps with learning, translation, and creativity (e.g. writing, art).
Enables new services and jobs in tech and related fields.
argumentsOtherSide: Privacy concerns: data collection and surveillance.
Job displacement in some sectors; need for retraining.
Bias and errors in AI can reinforce inequality or cause harm.
Over-reliance may reduce human skills and critical thinking.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: The government should provide free university education for all citizens.
argumentsOneSide: Education is a right; cost should not block talented students.
A more educated workforce benefits the economy and society.
Reduces inequality and gives everyone a fairer chance.
Many countries already offer free or low-cost higher education successfully.
argumentsOtherSide: Taxpayers may not want to fund degrees that do not lead to clear public benefit.
Free tuition could lead to overcrowding or lower quality if not managed well.
Other priorities (e.g. healthcare, schools) may need funding more.
Loans and scholarships can target those in need without making all free.

29.prompt_en: Why do some people prefer to live alone rather than with family or roommates?
argumentsOneSide: More freedom and control over schedule, space, and habits.
Privacy and quiet; better for focus (work or study) and mental rest.
Avoid conflicts or compromise on daily decisions.
Modern life and remote work make solo living more feasible and accepted.
argumentsOtherSide: Living with others can reduce loneliness and provide support.
Sharing costs (rent, utilities) makes housing more affordable.
Family or cultural values may emphasize living together.
Safety and practical help (e.g. illness, emergencies) are easier with others.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: Museums and galleries should be free for everyone.
argumentsOneSide: Culture and art should be accessible regardless of income.
Free entry increases attendance and educates the public.
Many museums receive public funding; they serve the public good.
Tourism and local economy can benefit from higher footfall.
argumentsOtherSide: Museums need revenue to maintain collections and pay staff.
A small fee can reduce overcrowding and fund special exhibitions.
Free entry for some (e.g. students, seniors) can balance access and revenue.
People may value the experience more when they pay something.

29.prompt_en: What are the advantages and disadvantages of living in a multicultural society?
argumentsOneSide: Exposure to different foods, languages, and traditions enriches life.
Diverse perspectives can improve creativity and problem-solving.
Reduces prejudice when people interact across cultures.
Economy can benefit from varied skills and international links.
argumentsOtherSide: Language barriers and misunderstandings can cause tension.
Some fear loss of traditional identity or values.
Integration and equal opportunity require resources and policy.
Conflict can arise over customs, religion, or political views.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: Technology makes people lonelier.
argumentsOneSide: Screen time can replace face-to-face interaction and deepen isolation.
Social media can increase comparison and anxiety rather than connection.
Working and studying from home reduce casual social contact.
Superficial online contact may not satisfy the need for real relationships.
argumentsOtherSide: Technology helps people stay in touch across distance (e.g. video calls).
Online communities can provide support for niche interests or difficulties.
Loneliness depends on how we use technology, not technology itself.
Apps and platforms can facilitate meeting people and organizing events.

29.prompt_en: Which is more effective: learning from books and lectures or learning by doing (e.g. projects, internships)?
argumentsOneSide: Books and lectures give structured knowledge and theory efficiently.
Experts can explain complex ideas and correct misconceptions.
Scales well: one teacher can reach many students.
Foundation in theory is needed before safe or effective practice in some fields.
argumentsOtherSide: Doing reinforces learning and builds skills that theory alone cannot.
Motivation is often higher when the task is real and meaningful.
Mistakes in practice lead to deeper understanding.
Employers value experience; internships and projects prepare for jobs.

29.prompt_en: Why should governments invest in renewable energy instead of fossil fuels?
argumentsOneSide: Renewable energy reduces greenhouse gas emissions and slows climate change.
Creates new jobs in manufacturing, installation, and research.
Reduces dependence on imported oil and gas; more energy security.
Long-term cost of renewables is falling; fossil fuels have hidden costs (health, environment).
argumentsOtherSide: Transition is expensive and may raise energy prices in the short term.
Some regions depend on fossil fuel jobs; transition must be managed.
Renewables are not always reliable (e.g. wind, sun); storage and backup needed.
Developing countries may need affordable energy to grow; fossil fuels are still cheaper in some places.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: Advertising does more harm than good.
argumentsOneSide: Encourages overconsumption and debt; creates unnecessary wants.
Can be misleading and exploit vulnerable people (e.g. children, elderly).
Invades privacy and contributes to information overload.
Promotes unhealthy products (e.g. junk food, alcohol) and unrealistic body images.
argumentsOtherSide: Informs consumers about new products and competitive prices.
Funds free content (e.g. TV, websites, apps).
Can promote positive behavior (e.g. health campaigns, charity).
Helps businesses grow and create jobs.

29.prompt_en: What are the advantages and disadvantages of strict laws on pollution?
argumentsOneSide: Clear rules push companies to innovate and reduce harm.
Improves public health and quality of life (air, water).
Creates a level playing field so responsible firms are not undercut.
Protects ecosystems and future generations.
argumentsOtherSide: Strict laws may raise costs and hurt competitiveness or jobs.
Enforcement can be difficult and expensive.
Some industries may move to countries with weaker rules (leakage).
Balance needed so that transition is gradual and affordable.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: People today rely too much on technology.
argumentsOneSide: Basic skills (e.g. mental math, map-reading) are being lost.
Overuse can harm health (posture, sleep, attention).
Critical systems (e.g. power, finance) are vulnerable to hacking or failure.
Human connection and patience may decrease.
argumentsOtherSide: Technology solves real problems (e.g. medical, communication, efficiency).
Relying on tools is normal; the goal is to use them wisely.
Restricting technology would disadvantage those who depend on it (e.g. disabled, remote workers).
Education and habits can address overuse without rejecting technology.

29.prompt_en: Which is more important for a country: economic growth or protecting the environment?
argumentsOneSide: Economic growth provides jobs, tax revenue, and funds for public services.
Wealthier societies can afford to invest in green technology later.
Poverty and unemployment cause suffering; growth can reduce them.
Innovation driven by growth can also lead to cleaner solutions.
argumentsOtherSide: Environmental damage (climate, pollution) threatens health and stability.
Clean air, water, and ecosystems have value that is not captured by GDP.
Sustainable practices can also create jobs and long-term growth.
Future generations depend on decisions made today.

29.prompt_en: Why do some people choose to change careers in the middle of their life?
argumentsOneSide: Seek better pay, work-life balance, or meaning.
Industry decline or burnout pushes them to look elsewhere.
New skills or interests develop over time.
Remote work and training make switching more feasible than before.
argumentsOtherSide: Some prefer stability and seniority in one field.
Switching can mean lower income or status in the short term.
Family and financial commitments may limit flexibility.
Employers may prefer younger or more specialized candidates in the new field.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: Schools should teach financial literacy (e.g. budgeting, saving) as a required subject.
argumentsOneSide: Many adults struggle with debt and poor financial decisions; early education helps.
Prepares students for real life (rent, taxes, loans, investments).
Can reduce inequality by giving everyone basic money skills.
Other life skills (e.g. cooking, civics) are already taught; money is equally important.
argumentsOtherSide: Curriculum is already crowded; other subjects might be cut.
Teachers may not be trained to teach finance well.
Family and culture also shape habits; school cannot fix everything.
Some topics (e.g. investing) may be better for older or voluntary courses.

29.prompt_en: What role should the government play in supporting the arts?
argumentsOneSide: Arts enrich society and should be accessible; government can fund museums, grants.
Creative industries create jobs and attract tourism.
Education in the arts develops creativity and critical thinking.
Public funding can support work that the market would not (e.g. experimental, local).
argumentsOtherSide: Government funding may lead to censorship or political influence.
Tax money might be better spent on health, education, or infrastructure.
Private donors and ticket sales can support the arts without the state.
Defining "art" and allocating funds fairly is difficult and subjective.

29.prompt_en: Why do people value traveling to other countries?
argumentsOneSide: Experience new cultures, languages, and perspectives; broadens the mind.
Break from routine; relaxation and adventure.
Builds memories and relationships; shared experiences with family or friends.
Economic and cultural exchange can foster understanding and peace.
argumentsOtherSide: Travel can be expensive and time-consuming; not everyone can afford it.
Tourism can harm local environments and cultures if not managed.
Some prefer to explore their own country or invest in home.
Carbon footprint of flying is a concern for many.

29.prompt_en: Do you agree or disagree: Celebrities and influencers have too much influence on young people.
argumentsOneSide: Young people may copy unhealthy behavior (e.g. diet, spending) or unrealistic standards.
Time spent following influencers could be used for learning or real relationships.
Sponsored content is often not clearly labeled; can be misleading.
Fame and wealth are overvalued; other role models (e.g. teachers, scientists) are underrated.
argumentsOtherSide: Influencers can spread positive messages (e.g. fitness, charity, education).
Young people can distinguish between entertainment and reality when guided.
Influence has always existed (e.g. stars, athletes); format has changed.
Parents and schools share responsibility for teaching critical thinking.

29.prompt_en: What are the advantages and disadvantages of a four-day work week?
argumentsOneSide: Better work-life balance and well-being; less burnout.
Can increase productivity (rested, focused employees).
Reduces commuting and can lower carbon emissions.
Attracts talent and may reduce absenteeism.
argumentsOtherSide: Some industries (e.g. healthcare, retail) need coverage every day.
Same pay for fewer hours may raise costs for employers.
Employees might work longer on the four days, reducing the benefit.
Coordination with clients or global teams could be harder.
